data.source: Online surveys of American participants (MTurk for Study 1; online samples for Studies 2 and 3)
data.wave_or_subset: Study 1 collected March 19–20, 2020; 193 of 346 had prior data from October 2019 enabling longitudinal analysis. Study 3 used a one-week longitudinal design (T1 to T2).
data.sample_size: Total N = 1,195 across three studies (Study 1: N = 346, with 193 longitudinal; Study 2: N = 537; Study 3: not stated).
data.notes: Self-reported measures from U.S.-based online samples; cross-sectional and short-term longitudinal designs; included the 10-item Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale alongside standard measures of anxiety, affect, life satisfaction, and COVID-19 impact (intrusion/avoidance).

method.description: Across three studies, participants completed the Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale assessing symbolic and realistic COVID-19 threats, along with measures of psychological distress, well-being, and public health behaviors. The scale’s structure and validity were examined using principal component analysis, confirmatory factor analysis, and longitudinal invariance testing. Structural equation modeling assessed criterion validity linking perceived threats to outcomes such as anxiety, intrusion/avoidance, life satisfaction, identity-affirming behaviors, and adherence to CDC guidelines.
steps: Recruit U.S. participants for online surveys (Study 1 via MTurk; Studies 2 and 3 online samples).

Administer the 10-item Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale (5 symbolic, 5 realistic) and outcome measures (e.g., anxiety, affect, intrusion/avoidance, life satisfaction, adherence to CDC guidelines, support for policies, identity-affirming behaviors).
Compute reliability for subscales and outcomes.
Examine factor structure using principal component analysis (two-factor solution).
Conduct confirmatory factor analysis comparing one-factor vs. two-factor models.
For longitudinal data, test configural, metric, and scalar invariance across time.
Assess convergent and divergent validity via correlations with constructs (e.g., need for cognitive closure, belief in a dangerous world, values, personality).
Use structural equation modeling to estimate associations between symbolic/realistic threats and criterion outcomes (distress, well-being, compliance).
For longitudinal analyses, predict T2 outcomes from T1 threats (controlling for baseline where available).
Conduct supplementary analyses including political ideology where applicable.
method.models: Principal component analysis; confirmatory factor analysis; structural equation modeling; longitudinal measurement invariance testing (configural, metric, scalar).
method.outcome_variable: Psychological distress (anxiety, negative affect, intrusion, avoidance), psychological well-being (life satisfaction, positive affect), public health compliance (adherence to CDC guidelines, support for restrictive policies), and social identity-affirming behaviors.
method.independent_variables: Perceived COVID-19 realistic threat; perceived COVID-19 symbolic threat (Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale).
method.control_variables: Pre-pandemic anxiety levels in longitudinal analyses; political ideology in supplementary analyses; other controls not stated.

results.summary: Across studies, symbolic and realistic COVID-19 threats showed distinct associations with psychological and behavioral outcomes. Realistic threat was robustly linked to greater anxiety and intrusive thoughts and predicted higher adherence to CDC guidelines and support for public health measures, but lower life satisfaction. Symbolic threat related to greater avoidance and intrusion, lower compliance with public health measures, and increased engagement in identity-affirming behaviors; it was also associated with positive affect via identity affirmation. Longitudinal analyses confirmed that both threats at T1 predicted increased distress at T2, with realistic threat uniquely reducing life satisfaction and strengthening compliance.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Adherence to CDC guidelines (Study 2; predictor: realistic threat)
Anxiety at T2 (Study 3; predictor at T1: realistic threat)
Anxiety at T2 (Study 3; predictor at T1: symbolic threat)
Anxiety (longitudinal replication; predictor: realistic threat controlling for pre-pandemic anxiety)
